name: Jean Baudrillard
topicalDescription: Jean Baudrillard wrote about the economic order but also about mass communication in the post modern age. Drawing on the work of Marshall McLuhan, Baurdillard came to claim that the current age was defined by simulation: a kind of “hyperreality” where eveything is a copy without an original.  Baudrillard, much like Francis Fukuyama, also thought that history had ended or “vanished,” but unlike Fukuyama, Baurdillard did not see this as the culmination of history’s progress but the end of the very idea of historical progress.
desc: Jean Baudrillard (UK: , US: ; French: [ʒɑ̃ bodʁijaʁ]; 27 July 1929 – 6 March 2007) was a French Marxist sociologist and philosopher with an interest in cultural studies. He is best known for his analyses of media, contemporary culture, and technological communication, as well as his formulation of concepts such as hyperreality. Baudrillard wrote about diverse subjects, including consumerism, critique of economy, social history, aesthetics, Western foreign policy, and popular culture. Among his best-known works are Forget Foucault (1977),  Seduction (1978), Simulacra and Simulation (1981), America (1986), and The Gulf War Did Not Take Place (1991). His work is frequently associated with postmodernism and specifically post-structuralism. Nevertheless, Baudrillard had also opposed post-structuralism, and had distanced himself from postmodernism.
